title: ERRO[0000] XDG_RUNTIME_DIR directory "/run/user/####" is not owned by the current user
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

When creating a new user and enabling linger the following error will be presented.
`ERRO[0000] XDG_RUNTIME_DIR directory "/run/user/<UID>" is not owned by the current user`

Most research links this to environment leaking when using su, but this also occurs when logging in from the console.

It appears to resolve itself upon a reboot, however, in a multi-tenant environment this would be unacceptable.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create new user
2. Enable Lingering for this user
3. Login to this user from the console
4. Run `podman info --log-level debug`

**Describe the results you received:**

XDG_RUNTIME_DIR error preventing usage without a reboot.

**Describe the results you expected:**

Podman should recognize the XDG_RUNTIME_DIR and operate normally in rootless mode.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

I have been able to replicate this without fail every time.
As Debian does not provide the latest version I have been using this [REPO ](https://build.opensuse.org/project/show/home:alvistack) and the maintainer has been excellent at working with me on any issues.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
WARN[0000] "/" is not a shared mount, this could cause issues or missing mounts with rootless containers 
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.3
API Version:  4.0.3
Go Version:   go1.18
Built:        Wed Dec 31 18:00:00 1969
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
WARN[0000] Failed to add pause process to systemd sandbox cgroup: exec: "dbus-launch": executable file not found in $PATH
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**
This output is from a user that does not have lingering enabled as I am unable to run this command as such.
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**
As mentioned, I am using a 3rd party repo, the `100:` is just a prefix to the upstream version.
```
podman/unknown,now 100:4.0.3-1 amd64 [installed]
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Running Debian Bullseye on LXC on Proxmox.

```
PRETTY_NAME="Debian GNU/Linux 11 (bullseye)"
NAME="Debian GNU/Linux"
VERSION_ID="11"
VERSION="11 (bullseye)"
VERSION_CODENAME=bullseye
ID=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.debian.org/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://www.debian.org/support"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.debian.org/"
```

I was pointed to [`util_supported.go`](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/112a47964ddbe816349ea412c0073902e805f943/pkg/util/util_supported.go#L41-L42) as the source of the error message.

I replicated the function as a test and am unable to produce the error.

Code to test:
```
package main

import (
        "fmt"
        "os"
        "syscall"
)

func main() {
  runtimeDir := os.Getenv("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR")
  fmt.Printf("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR: %s\n", runtimeDir)
  if runtimeDir != "" {
    st, err := os.Stat(runtimeDir)
    if err != nil {
      fmt.Printf("Could not stat (%s): %v", runtimeDir, err)
      os.Exit(1)
    }
    if int(st.Sys().(*syscall.Stat_t).Uid) != os.Geteuid() {
      fmt.Printf("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR directory %q is not owned by the current user", runtimeDir)
      os.Exit(2)
    }
    stat := st.Sys().(*syscall.Stat_t)
    fmt.Printf("Dir: (%s) Owner: (%d) Group: (%d)\n", st.Name(), stat.Uid, stat.Gid)
  }
}
```
Output via console direct login:
```
podmanone@2006-ct:~/gotest$ id && go run main.go
uid=1002(podmanone) gid=1002(podmanone) groups=1002(podmanone)
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR: /run/user/1002
Dir: (1002) Owner: (1002) Group: (1002)
```
Output via `sudo su - podmanone`:
```
podmanone@2006-ct:~/gotest$ id && go run main.go 
uid=1002(podmanone) gid=1002(podmanone) groups=1002(podmanone)
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR: /run/user/1002
Dir: (1002) Owner: (1002) Group: (1002)
```

comments.body: How are you logging into the user? Some login methods (`su`, `sudo`) do not appropriately update environment variables, so you can end up with XDG_RUNTIME_DIR pointing to the wrong place.

I think the problem here is something with the userns.

The env is correctly at the moment you check but it was likely not correct before.
Please check `cat /proc/$(pgrep catatonit)/uid_map`

Also I think it is expected the lingering only works after a reboot. Did you try using `machinectl shell @username` this should create a proper systemd session.

@mheon I am logging in via the console directly, not as another user then changing over to the lingering user.

I also have set this in my `.bashrc`
```
export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR="${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR:-/run/user/$UID}"
export DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS="${DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS:-unix:path=${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/bus}"
```

@Luap99 I am unable to run your machinectl command, and I'm not really familiar with that command. From my brief understanding, it is for working with Systemd containers?
```
austin@2006-ct:~$ sudo machinectl shell @podmanone
Failed to parse machine specification: Invalid argument
```

Also, for your catatonit, I get multiple entries.
```
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ pgrep catatonit
9370
9767
10121
21937
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /proc/9370/uid_map
         0          0 4294967295
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /proc/9767/uid_map
         0          0 4294967295
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /proc/10121/uid_map
         0       1002          1
         1     231072      65536
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /proc/21937/uid_map
         0       1000          1
         1     100000      65536
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /etc/subuid
ansible:100000:65536
austin:165536:65536
podmanone:231072:65536
podmantwo:296608:65536
podmanthree:362144:65536
podmanfour:427680:65536
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /etc/subgid
ansible:100000:65536
austin:165536:65536
podmanone:231072:65536
podmantwo:296608:65536
podmanthree:362144:65536
podmanfour:427680:65536
```
sorry the command is `machinectl shell username@`

Unfortunately no change.

```
austin@2006-ct:~$ sudo machinectl shell podmanone@
Connected to the local host. Press ^] three times within 1s to exit session.
podmanone@2006-ct:~/gotest$ go run main.go
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR: /run/user/1002
Dir: (1002) Owner: (1002) Group: (1002)
podmanone@2006-ct:~/gotest$ podman info
ERRO[0000] XDG_RUNTIME_DIR directory "/run/user/1002" is not owned by the current user
```
ls -ld /run/user/1002

```
$ ls -ld /run/user/1002
drwx------ 6 podmanone podmanone 160 Apr 22 14:33 /run/user/1002
```
```
$ id -u podmanone       
1002
```
What does id show?

```
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ id
uid=1002(podmanone) gid=1002(podmanone) groups=1002(podmanone)
```

Weird.
Does `podman unshare` work?

That command scared me for a second until I read up more on it, I was not expecting a root prompt!

```
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ podman unshare
WARN[0000] "/" is not a shared mount, this could cause issues or missing mounts with rootless containers 
root@2006-ct:~# exit
```
Could you look at the ownership of /run/user/1002 here.  Also cat /proc/self/uid_map

```
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ stat /run/user/1002
  File: /run/user/1002
  Size: 180             Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   directory
Device: 100041h/1048641d        Inode: 1           Links: 7
Access: (0700/drwx------)  Uid: ( 1002/podmanone)   Gid: ( 1002/podmanone)
Access: 2022-04-22 14:33:28.190448913 -0500
Modify: 2022-05-02 17:38:12.760790347 -0500
Change: 2022-05-02 17:38:12.760790347 -0500
 Birth: -
```

```
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0          0 4294967295
```
Sorry I wanted that done while in the user namespace.  Something is tricking Podman to think it does not own that directory.

Ahh yes, that makes sense. I was also able to touch a test file in /run/user/1002 while in this mode.
```
podmanone@2006-ct:~$ podman unshare
root@2006-ct:~# stat /run/user/1002
  File: /run/user/1002
  Size: 180             Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   directory
Device: 100041h/1048641d        Inode: 1           Links: 7
Access: (0700/drwx------)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
Access: 2022-04-22 14:33:28.190448913 -0500
Modify: 2022-05-02 17:38:12.760790347 -0500
Change: 2022-05-02 17:38:12.760790347 -0500
 Birth: -
root@2006-ct:~# cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0       1002          1
         1     231072      65536
```
I have no idea what Podman is complaining about.  @mheon @giuseppe your guess is as good as mine.

that `WARN[0000] "/" is not a shared mount, this could cause issues or missing mounts with rootless containers ` doesn't look right.  It might cause some mounts to be missing in the rootless user namespace including the `/run/user/$UID` tmpfs.

Could you show me the output of `podman unshare cat /proc/self/mountinfo`?

Can you also try recreating the rootless user namespace with `podman system migrate`  Does the error go away?

I'll add that this machine is an LXC on Proxmox.

```

So it looks like things are working after `podman system migrate`.
I created another user and verified that `podman info` worked.
Then I enabled lingering for that user and verified that it was working again.
I am still getting the error about the shared mount, but I am able to run the `hello-world` image successfully.

So in summary, it looks like running `podman system migrate` fixes the issue for the entire machine.

I wanted to verify that this is repeatable, and unfortunately I can't get it to work again.
I rebuilt the machine and podman info, unshare, run all fail with `ERRO[0000] XDG_RUNTIME_DIR directory "/run/user/1002" is not owned by the current user`.
Even `podman system migrate` gives this error now.

I'll do a bit more experimenting on my end, but curious what your thoughts are.

I think the problem is related to `/` not being shared.

In the podman unshare environment there is no mount for `/run/user/1002` so that directory is in fact not writeable.

You could fix with `sudo mount --make-rshared / && podman system migrate`

Can you try that?

I ran that command as a user with sudo privilege, but my linger user that does not have sudo still shows the XDG error.
I am not seeing the error about the shared mount. I will try adding that as a step to my Ansible playbook and rebuild to see if there is any change.

I'm seeing an issue that might be related to this: trying to execute `podman` *within* a gitlab-runner I got the same error (about `/run/user/107` not being writable, while I checked on the host and it was owned by `gitlab-runner` and writable.

Thinking to be smart I forced `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=${HOME}/.run` for the whole shell runner environment (and I ensure the existence of that dir), but I still get an error about the "old" runtime dir:

```
time="2022-05-11T17:06:55+02:00" level=warning msg="XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is pointing to a path which is not writable. Most likely podman will fail.: read-only file system"
Error: error creating tmpdir: mkdir /run/user/107/libpod: read-only file system
```
That seems to mean that somewhere `podman` is not actually honoring `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` all the way?

@cevich PTAL

@rhatdan this is a bit far outside my knowledge domain, but I read through the comments nonetheless.  What strikes me is the mention of `I'll add that this machine is an LXC on Proxmox.` and `trying to execute podman within a gitlab-runner I got the same error`.  Then @giuseppe comment `I think the problem is related to / not being shared.`  Another common theme here seems to be seccomp:  Running podman under docker (gitlab case) and podman under LXC.  So this is container-in-container, and we've seen countless examples of various problems when the "host" (a container) has an overly restrictive seccomp profile.  I forget the term, but I vaguely remember something related to 'mount' and the default docker profile denying it.  Perhaps LXC does something similar?
Very likely.  Docker remove the unshare and mount syscall by default.  While Podman does not.
